food_subgroup: Pork bone broth soup with vegetables and aromatics

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry exists for Kayin Talabaw. FOODON_00002482 (pork soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pork, cooked, as soup or stew
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (pork, cooked) used as closest parent; no specific Karen pork rib soup entry exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Kayin Talabaw or Karen pork rib soup. SNOMED CT does not catalogue ethnic-specific soup preparations at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Kayin Talabaw is approximately 400 g, comprising pork ribs, broth, and aromatics as typically served at a Singaporean café or Karen food stall.

nutrient_source.title: Pork ribs, cooked, braised — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10124); supplemented with USDA broth and aromatic ingredient data for composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Kayin Talabaw is not present in any primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for braised pork ribs (NDB 10124) as the dominant macronutrient contributor, adjusted for dilution by broth (~50% pork ribs, ~50% broth and aromatics by weight). This is a composite estimation. Values should be treated as indicative pending laboratory analysis of the specific dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of meat-based soups and broths — analogous estimate based on pork-dominant low-carbohydrate soup; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kayin Talabaw. GI estimated at 30 based on analogous low-carbohydrate pork-based soups and broths. Meat-dominant soups with minimal starch have consistently low GI values (typically 20–40) in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (30 × 1.2 × 4) / 100 = 1.44, rounded to 1.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from fish sauce and broth
hypertension.guidance: Each 400 g bowl provides approximately 1280 mg sodium, representing over 50% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and limit portion size or broth consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With approximately 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 30, Kayin Talabaw has a negligible glycemic load and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added rice or starchy accompaniments.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork ribs and moderate sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat content (~2.6 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium (~320 mg per 100 g) are relevant for cardiovascular risk management; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and prefer leaner cuts where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kayin Talabaw (ကရင်တာလပေါ့) is a traditional pork rib soup originating from the Karen (Kayin) ethnic group of Myanmar. The dish is prepared by slow-simmering pork ribs and bones with aromatic ingredients including lemongrass, galangal, garlic, shallots, and fish sauce, producing a deeply flavoured, clear-to-lightly-cloudy broth. The soup is characterised by its savoury, mildly herbaceous taste with a rich pork umami base and a subtle heat from aromatics. The texture features tender, fall-off-the-bone pork ribs in a light but flavourful broth. In Singapore, Kayin Talabaw is served at Karen community food stalls and select cafés such as The Light Cafe at High Street Plaza, reflecting the presence of the Karen diaspora community. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (~10 g per 100 g) and moderate in fat (~7 g per 100 g), with very low carbohydrate content (~1.2 g per 100 g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce and broth concentration, which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated to be low (~30), making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice. The dish is gluten-free and dairy-free by default.

confidence_reason: Kayin Talabaw is not present in any approved primary regional nutrition database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for braised pork ribs as the closest defensible analogue, adjusted for broth dilution. GI is estimated from analogous low-carbohydrate pork-based soups in the University of Sydney GI Database literature. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation rather than direct measurement.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10124, braised pork ribs) adjusted for approximately 50% dilution by broth and aromatics. These values are indicative and should be validated by laboratory analysis of the specific dish preparation. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food category. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the composite estimation source. 3. GI is estimated at 30 based on analogous low-carbohydrate pork-dominant soups; no direct GI measurement for Kayin Talabaw exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. 4. The cuisine tag 'Italian' in the input JSON is incorrect; the dish is Karen/Burmese in origin and has been reclassified accordingly. The restaurant (The Light Cafe, High Street Plaza, Singapore) lists Italian and other cuisine tags on field survey, but Kayin Talabaw is a Karen ethnic dish. 5. Halal status is false as the dish contains pork. 6. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi have been provided following Singaporean dish multilingual requirements, as this dish is served in Singapore. 7. The input food_id GD004012 is preserved as the record identifier.
